page: UpCloud 2xCPU-4GB is a general-purpose virtual server built on the KVM hypervisor. It features 2 shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture. The hardware configuration includes 4.0 GB of system memory, providing 2.0 GB per core, alongside 80 GB of local SSD storage. No dedicated GPU is included. It also comes with one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Performance benchmarks indicate that this instance is positioned at the entry level. Stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU tests place the server in the bottom 10% of performance, with scores of 779.78 and 1536.77 respectively. Memory bandwidth is similarly constrained, with large-block read-only speeds at 14.52 GB/sec and write speeds at 10.94 GB/sec, both falling into the bottom 10%. However, single-threaded tasks show moderate capability; gzip compression and bzip3 decompression single-threaded performance both rank in the middle 50% tier. Geekbench single-core scores also reach an average tier at 1166.0, though multi-core operations and software compilation remain weak.

Given the shared CPU allocation and entry-level benchmark results, this server offers a highly cost-efficient profile for workloads that do not require sustained high-performance compute or massive memory throughput. It is best suited for lightweight applications, basic web serving, small-scale development environments, background utility scripts, and testing. The bundled 80 GB SSD provides sufficient local storage for operating system files and modest application data, making it a practical choice for budget-conscious hosting scenarios.
description: The UpCloud 2xCPU-4GB is an entry-level, general-purpose virtual server utilizing the KVM hypervisor. It is equipped with 2 shared vCPUs from an AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz on the x86_64 architecture, paired with 4.0 GB of RAM and 80 GB of local SSD storage. Benchmark data indicates modest performance, with CPU and memory bandwidth metrics generally falling into the lower percentiles, though single-threaded tasks like gzip compression and Geekbench single-core tests achieve average tier results. Lacking a dedicated GPU, this resource-constrained instance is qualitatively cost-efficient for non-demanding workloads. It is best suited for lightweight web serving, development environments, testing, and basic background utility tasks.
